Samson's life is a study in the terrible consequences of unchecked impulse masked as righteousness. When his wife is given to another man, he declares that "this time I will be blameless in doing harm to the Philistines"—a statement that reveals the deepest problem with Samson. He is not fighting for Israel's freedom or God's glory. He is avenging his personal humiliation. Three hundred foxes with torches, grain burning across Philistine fields, the retaliatory murder of his wife and her father—these are not the actions of God's deliverer but of a man consumed with rage. The tragedy deepens when the Philistines, seeking Samson's blood, camp in Judah itself. The very people Samson claims to be saving turn against him, begging him to understand what his vendetta has cost them. His own countrymen must bind him and hand him over. This is what Samson has become: a liability to Israel, a man whose personal grievances have made his people enslaved and afraid.

Yet God has not abandoned Samson, even in his self-destruction. When the Philistines come to seize him at Lehi, "the Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him." The ropes dissolve like burnt flax, and with nothing but a donkey's jawbone, Samson slays a thousand men. Here is the paradox that defines his entire story: God uses Samson despite himself, granting supernatural power to a man driven by vengeance rather than obedience. Samson knows this, at least in part. After his great slaughter, he cries out to the Lord, acknowledging that God "accomplished this great deliverance." But notice what he does with that acknowledgment—he uses it to demand that God sustain him, almost as though his thirst is God's failure. He has not repented. He is simply reminding the Lord of services rendered.

When God provides water from the hollow place in Lehi, refreshing Samson after his killing spree, we are confronted with a God who acts according to His purposes, not according to our worthiness. Samson is delivered, sustained, and even used mightily, but he remains what he has always been: a man ruled by appetite, anger, and self-interest. He judges Israel for twenty years, yet his judgeship accomplishes little lasting freedom. He is a tool in God's hand, but a broken one, and the text never lets us forget it. The real question is not whether Samson will succeed, but whether we will recognize ourselves in him—in our eagerness to dress up our anger as righteousness, our self-interest as service, our demand that God bless our passions as though He owed us His approval.
